FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 at baseline, n=10,317 participants from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with logistic regression examining associations between ACEs and cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescence

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization alone as dependent variable paired with ACEs as predictor. Per v7 binding clarification (§2.4), cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable for IC2 purposes; no separate phone/SM measure with own coefficient reported.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 on binding rule for cyberbullying-alone outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14, n=9,443 participants

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression to examine associations between cyberbullying victimization/perpetration and sleep disturbance

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as independent variables paired only with sleep disturbance as outcome. Per v7 binding clarification (§2.4), cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable; no phone/SM measure appears as IV or DV in any analysis.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 on binding rule for cyberbullying-alone as IV without paired phone/SM measure

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14, n=10,339 participants from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with multiple linear regression examining cross-sectional associations between sexual orientation and screen use modalities

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Annals of Epidemiology

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (broad search): Multiple phone/SM-specific variables found with numeric estimates paired with individual-trait variable (sexual orientation/identity). Main results Table 2 reports: recreational screen time β=3.72 (95% CI 2.96-4.47) more hours daily for sexual minority vs. heterosexual; MPIQ (mobile phone problematic use) β=0.56 (95% CI 0.41-0.70) higher; SMAQ (social media problematic use) β=0.46 (95% CI 0.32-0.60) higher; VGAQ (video game addiction) β=0.49 (95% CI 0.33-0.64) higher. Sexual orientation/gender identity qualifies as individual-trait-level per v7 (§2.4), not demographic. Other side meets individual-trait requirement.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported throughout: β values for sexual minority vs. heterosexual comparisons with 95% CIs for total screen time, television, videos, gaming (single/multi-player), texting, social media, video chat, and internet browsing (Table 2). Adjusted models control for confounders.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported for SM/phone ↔ individual-trait associations in main and supplementary analyses; β coefficients and 95% CIs provided for all associations

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 2 ABCD data analyzed cross-sectionally); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal SM-as-DV-only papers. This paper is cross-sectional, so EC7 does not apply regardless of SM side of equation.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria and does not trigger FT-EC7 exclusion due to cross-sectional design
